segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black screen before transitioning to a polished nighttime studio setting, typical of a late-night talk show monologue. A male host, Jimmy Kimmel, dressed in a dark suit with a white shirt and a deep purple tie, stands center stage. He begins with his hands clasped, then gradually becomes more expressive, opening them outward for emphasis as he speaks in a solemn, direct tone. Behind him, a vibrant, illuminated cityscape backdrop features the iconic 'HOLLYWOOD' sign in the distance, suggesting a Los Angeles location. On-screen graphics display a 'TV 14 DL' rating in the top left and the 'Jimmy Kimmel Live!' logo in a purple circle in the bottom left. Jimmy Kimmel directly addresses the audience, stating that tonight's show will be different due to the absence of a studio audience, as a comedy show didn't feel right after yesterday's events. He then somberly announces the tragic death of Kobe Bryant, his daughter Gianna, and seven friends in a helicopter crash in Los Angeles, describing it as 'a punch in the gut.' He reflects on Kobe as 'the last person you could ever imagine something like this happening to,' highlighting his strength, handsomeness, intelligence, and energy, calling him a 'hero' while clarifying he doesn't compare him to 'real heroes' like firefighters or doctors, acknowledging that 'there are more important things than basketball.'

He speaks about a superstar athlete who, unlike almost every other, never left, playing his 20-year career in only two uniforms for the Lakers and the US Olympic team. Kimmel notes that though it seemed he might leave, he stayed until the end, loved by almost everyone in the city, not just Lakers fans, who would light up upon seeing him. He describes the athlete as a special person, a great basketball player, but also intensely curious with many interests outside of sports, who would call business leaders, writers, and inventors he admired to interview them, always learning and improving with a hunger for knowledge. Kimmel then recounts sending the athlete a book after he tore his Achilles, and to his surprise, the athlete read it and called him to discuss it in 'maniacal detail.' Kimmel humorously recalls telling him, 'I don't know Kobe, I didn't write it, I just bought the book,' and having to connect him with the author.

Kimmel continues the jovial tone by jokingly announcing that "Pau got deported," eliciting more laughter. He then turns to Kobe, recalling his earlier statement that the opponent (Celtics) didn't matter, only the series itself, before transitioning to a brief clip. The camera cuts to a live ESPN News graphic showing 'NBA FINALS Game 7: Celtics at Lakers' as a reporter's voice asks about the significance of being in Game 7 of the finals. Behind them, a nighttime Hollywood cityscape backdrop features the iconic 'HOLLYWOOD' sign, while a large screen proudly displays '2010 NBA Champions Lakers'. Look at this guy," referring to the bald guest, as the audience erupts in cheers and applause. Kimmel continues, recalling a past conversation with Kobe about the Celtics' previous victory, emphasizing Kobe's intense desire to win: "You were very, you were very upset that the Celtics beat you... I would honestly, I would have bet my... everything... that you were gonna win because when we spoke about it, you were so serious and so intense about it. It scared me a little." Kobe confirms, "Well, we were upset. We were upset. I mean, Derek and I..." identifying the bald guest as Derek. Kimmel then playfully interjects, "Derek didn't care as much, I don't think," prompting Kobe to retort with a smile, "He sits here, he looks so well-mannered and all. He's got a quicker temper than I do," as Kimmel addresses Derek directly.

The host laughs, adding, "You hate to make her break her crayons out and make it silver after," as Kobe smiles, adjusting his tie and displaying a large ring on his finger. Kobe concludes, reflecting on the unique nature of children, stating, "it's crazy, you know, I have to tell you, man, it's what the thing about the thing about kids, the thing about kids, man, is that you know, you don't want you don't want..." The atmosphere remains warm and conversational, with both men laughing and interacting comfortably as the camera alternates between close-ups of Kobe and wider shots.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a close-up of Kobe Bryant, dressed in a dark gray checkered suit, white shirt, and red pocket square, seated on a gray couch in a studio setting. How involved were you in in LeBron coming to LA? Did you speak to him?" The backdrop features a nighttime cityscape with illuminated buildings and the iconic 'HOLLYWOOD' sign. Kobe replies, "No, I didn't speak to him about coming to LA, but I I spoke to Genie," which Kimmel clarifies as "To Genie Buss? The owner of the team." So you have to figure out management, you have to make sure that things are clean, there's a focus going forward, it's not chaotic as it has been in years past. And uh, and she did that." Kimmel acknowledges with "Mhm" and "Gotcha." The two men continue their dynamic interaction, with Kobe leaning forward and laughing at points, while Kimmel responds with enthusiasm, occasionally leaning back and smiling, shifting the topic: "And now yet here we are, three but you're you're worried about your own team, you're coaching your daughter's team." segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Kobe Bryant and Jimmy Kimmel in a studio setting, both laughing as Kobe, dressed in a dark gray checkered suit with a white shirt and a red pocket square, speaks expressively, using hand gestures to attribute a thought process to the "Mamba mentality." Jimmy, in a dark suit with a white shirt and dark purple tie, listens attentively, nodding, and then recounts a rigorous practice routine involving "running for two hours straight... no water breaks, nothing. Push-ups and sit-ups all day." He then asks Kobe about the seriousness of their training, inquiring if they're "breaking down game film," to which Kobe quickly replies, "Not yet, but we will." Jimmy acknowledges, and Kobe confirms, "Absolutely will." Kobe then elaborates on his coaching philosophy, stating, "we try to teach the kids what excellence looks like." A close-up on Kobe shows him speaking expressively as he explains that while some kids "may want to play in the WNBA, some of them may not," Jimmy interjects with "Right." Kobe continues, emphasizing the importance of providing "a foundation of the amount of work and preparation that it takes to be excellent in whatever it is that you choose to do." He gestures as he details their approach: "we're here playing basketball, we're going to focus on the details. We're going to learn the basics, we're going to learn the fundamentals. We're going to do those things over and over," concluding that he hopes it's "something that they can apply to other areas in their life." Jimmy then asks, "Do you think your daughter might want to play in the WNBA?" Kobe, smiling, confidently states, "She does for sure." Jimmy reacts with "She does," and Kobe gestures, adding, "I don't, I mean, this kid, man." Jimmy exclaims, "Wouldn't that be great?" and Kobe animatedly agrees, "Dude, man, I'm telling you." The segment concludes with applause and laughter, as both Kobe and Jimmy smile, clearly enjoying their engaging exchange against a backdrop of a nighttime cityscape with illuminated buildings and the 'HOLLYWOOD' sign.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a wide shot of a late-night talk show set, featuring Kobe Bryant seated on a gray couch and Jimmy Kimmel behind a polished wooden desk, both dressed in dark suits. Jimmy Kimmel, in a dark suit, white shirt, and dark purple tie, gestures expressively as he speaks to Kobe Bryant, who listens attentively, smiling and nodding. segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a wide shot of Kobe Bryant, a tall man with a shaved head, seated on a gray couch in a dark gray checkered suit, white shirt, and red pocket square, engaged in an animated conversation with Jimmy Kimmel, who is seated behind a polished wooden desk in a dark suit, white shirt, and dark purple tie. The backdrop features a softly lit nighttime cityscape with illuminated buildings and the iconic 'HOLLYWOOD' sign. Jimmy Kimmel, listening attentively and nodding, asks Kobe if any other player has done what he did, to which Kobe responds, “Not that I know of,” with a laugh. Kimmel finds this “absolutely not… interesting,” then clarifies, “In order to protect… your ankles, you learned to tap dance.” Kobe confirms, “I did,” prompting Kimmel to exclaim, “Why didn’t we know this?” as the audience laughs. Kobe, smiling and gesturing expressively, explains it was “kept secret for obvious reasons.” Kimmel then inquires if tap shoes are made in Kobe’s size, to which Kobe, still animated, states, “I had to get ‘em made,” adding, “I can’t go to the like a store and be like, ‘Hey, y’all take those size 14s?’” This elicits more audience laughter, and Kimmel jokes about calling Nike for tap shoes. As the camera alternates between wide shots and close-ups of Kobe’s facial expressions, Kimmel asks if Kobe is an “accomplished tapper.” Kobe emphatically replies, “God no,” though he admits, “for one summer, for a year there, yeah,” he “could like tell my feet to do this and they would actually do that.” Kimmel asks if he still does it, and Kobe responds, “Uh no, I don’t. I don’t,” before humorously recalling his first class in a Santa Monica studio filled with “six-year-old, seven-year-old” kids, while Kimmel listens and nods. The warm, professional studio lighting highlights the relaxed and conversational atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Kobe Bryant, in a white t-shirt and black shorts, shaking hands with a young boy on a basketball court amidst an enthusiastic crowd, as a male vocalist begins to sing 'There goes my hero' over an acoustic guitar. The scene transitions to Kobe in a purple Lakers jersey (number 24), clapping and smiling in a gym adorned with framed jerseys, followed by him dribbling a basketball and performing a powerful dunk in a home gym, then mid-air in a white tank top. The narrative shifts to a public tribute where a man and a child pose in front of a large 'THANK YOU 24' sign featuring Kobe’s image. Kobe, in his yellow Lakers jersey, walks through a crowd of fans, high-fiving and waving, with 'KOBE'S FAREWELL' and '#ThankYouKobe' displayed on the jumbotron, as the song continues with 'Watch him as he goes'. He is then seen sitting courtside with a young girl in a white beanie, both smiling, during a game with the score 'ATL 83, BKN 69' shown. The video cuts to Kobe in a black suit, proudly holding a gold Oscar statuette, then celebrating in a gray '2009 NBA Champions' t-shirt amidst falling confetti. A family moment follows, with Kobe in a suit, holding a baby, seated between his wife and two daughters at a game. He then stands on the court, holding a microphone and a towel, delivering a farewell speech with 'KOBE'S FAREWELL' and '#ThankYouKobe' on the jumbotron, as the song repeats 'There goes my hero' and adds 'He's ordinary'. The scene shifts to a black-and-white image of Kobe and a young girl laughing together, followed by a black screen with the quote: 'The most important thing is to try and inspire people so they can be great in whatever they want to do.' — Kobe Bryant, all while a crowd cheers loudly and a final buzzer sounds.
